# Therapy Session Transcript
**Session #4 | Client: Male, early 30s | Setting: Private practice office**

---

Dr: So, it's good to see you again. How have things been since we last spoke?

Client: Honestly? Not great. I mean, I've had better weeks. I don't even know where to start, really.

Dr: That's okay. There's no wrong place to start. Just say whatever's coming up for you right now.

Client: Okay. So... work has been really bad lately. Like, worse than usual. My manager keeps piling things on and then disappearing, and I'm left holding everything. And I know that sounds like a normal work complaint, but it's gotten to a point where I'm waking up at like three in the morning just... thinking about emails. About deadlines. It's like my brain won't turn off.

Dr: That sounds exhausting. When you wake up at three in the morning, what does that feel like in those moments?

Client: It's like... dread, almost. There's this immediate weight that drops on my chest. I'm not even fully awake yet and I'm already anxious. I'll just lie there staring at the ceiling, running through everything I haven't finished, everything that could go wrong. Sometimes I pick up my phone and start drafting responses at four in the morning, which I know is stupid.

Dr: Why do you say it's stupid?

Client: Because it doesn't help. I send these emails and nobody responds until nine anyway. And then I've just... burned through whatever sleep I had left. So I show up to work already running on empty and the whole cycle just repeats.

Dr: It sounds like there's a pattern here that you're very aware of, and yet it feels hard to break out of. What do you think is keeping you stuck in it?

Client: I think... I'm scared of falling behind. Like, genuinely scared. I grew up in a family where my dad lost his job when I was twelve and everything just fell apart. And I swore I'd never let that happen to me. So now even when something is a small thing at work, my brain treats it like it's this catastrophic threat. Like, if I drop one ball, everything collapses.

Dr: That's a really important connection you just made. That fear has roots that go way back. Can you tell me more about what happened when your dad lost his job? What was that like for you as a kid?

Client: It was... it was a lot. He didn't tell us right away, so there were weeks where everything seemed normal but something felt off. My mom was quieter. They'd stop talking when I walked into the room. And then one day I came home and we were selling furniture. Just like that. I remember feeling like the ground had been pulled out from under me and nobody had warned me. I think that's the thing. Nobody told me it was coming.

Dr: So part of what was most painful wasn't just the hardship, but the not knowing. The uncertainty.

Client: Yeah. Yeah, exactly. And I think that's why I can't let go of control now. If I'm on top of every single thing, then nothing can sneak up on me. But the problem is... I can't actually control everything. And the trying is killing me.

Dr: When you say it's killing you, what do you mean by that?

Client: I mean I'm tired. I'm really, really tired. I don't enjoy anything anymore. Like, last weekend my girlfriend wanted to go hiking, something I used to love, and I just... couldn't. I sat on the couch and I made excuses and she was disappointed and I felt guilty on top of everything else. I feel like I'm disappearing a little bit.

Dr: That phrase, disappearing, can you say more about that?

Client: Like I'm just... going through motions. I'm present but I'm not really there. I look at photos from a couple years ago and that person looks lighter. Happier. And I don't know how to get back to that. I'm not sure I believe I can, honestly.

Dr: I hear you saying you're not sure you believe it's possible. Is that a feeling that comes and goes, or does it feel more constant lately?

Client: More constant, I think. It's like background noise that used to come and go but now it's just... always on. I don't feel hopeless exactly. But I don't feel hopeful either. I feel kind of flat.

Dr: That flatness, that absence of feeling hopeful, that matters and I want us to spend more time with it. I also want to acknowledge how much you shared today, because some of that, the stuff about your dad, I don't think you've talked about that directly before.

Client: No, I haven't. It felt weird to say out loud. But also kind of right, I guess.

Dr: Good weird or uncomfortable weird?

Client: Both. Mostly good, I think. Like something that's been sitting in a drawer for a long time finally got opened.

Dr: That's a really good image. And we'll keep that drawer open. We don't have to rush. For now, I just want to ask, how are you feeling in this moment, right here?

Client: Tired. But a little less alone in it, maybe.

---

*[End of session excerpt — 6 min 40 sec]*

# Therapy Session Transcript
**Session Type:** Individual Psychotherapy — Initial Assessment
**Language:** English

---

Dr: So, come in, make yourself comfortable. There's no rush here. Can you start by just telling me a little bit about what brought you in today?

Client: Yeah, um... thanks. Sorry, I'm a little nervous. I don't really do this — talk to people about stuff. I guess... things have just been piling up lately and my sister basically forced me to make this appointment. She said I wasn't myself anymore and honestly I couldn't argue with her.

Dr: It sounds like it took some courage to get here. When you say things have been piling up — can you tell me more about what that looks like for you day to day?

Client: It's like... okay, so I wake up in the morning and the first thing I feel is this weight. Before I even open my eyes. Like I already know the day is going to be too much, you know? And then I go to work and I sit at my desk and I just... I stare at the screen. I used to be really good at my job. I was the person people came to. Now I can barely write an email without second-guessing every word for twenty minutes.

Dr: That sounds exhausting — carrying that weight from the moment you wake up. How long has it been feeling this way?

Client: Honestly? Probably six months. But I kept telling myself it was just a rough patch. A project deadline, a bad week, whatever. But the deadlines passed and it didn't go away. If anything it got worse. And I stopped going to the gym, I stopped seeing friends... I just go home and lie on the couch and watch things I've already seen because I don't have the energy to follow anything new.

Dr: I hear you. It sounds like things that used to feel natural or enjoyable have kind of... faded. What's going on with your sleep during this time?

Client: It's terrible. I fall asleep okay, which is almost worse because I think oh good, tonight will be different — and then I wake up at like 2 or 3am and that's it. I'm just lying there with my brain going at a hundred miles an hour. Going over conversations I had, things I should have done differently, worrying about stuff that might not even happen. Last week I was lying there at 3am genuinely convinced I was going to get fired, and there's no real reason for that. My boss hasn't said anything. But it felt absolutely real.

Dr: That kind of nighttime spiral can be really destabilizing. When those thoughts come — the ones about getting fired, or things going wrong — where do you feel that in your body?

Client: *(pauses)* That's... a weird question but actually yeah. My chest. It gets tight. And my jaw — I've started grinding my teeth apparently because my dentist told me I'd cracked a molar. I didn't even know I was doing it. It's like my body is stressed even when I'm asleep. Even when I'm supposed to be resting, I can't fully rest.

Dr: Your body is holding a lot. I want to ask about something you mentioned — that your sister said you weren't yourself. When you think about who you were before all this, what does that person feel like to you now?

Client: *(long pause)* Far away. Like... I can remember being that person but I can't really access her. She was funny. I used to be funny, I used to make people laugh. And I cared about things. I had this whole plan — I was going to apply for a promotion, I was thinking about maybe moving cities, trying new things. And now when I think about any of that it just feels... pointless? Not like I want to hurt myself or anything, I want to be clear about that. It's more like the future used to feel like something I was walking toward and now it just feels like a fog.

Dr: Thank you for saying that clearly — I did want to understand what you meant by pointless. That image you used, of the future feeling like a fog rather than something you're moving toward — that's really significant. When did that shift happen, do you think?

Client: I think... there was this one Sunday. Maybe eight months ago. I had a completely free day and I remember sitting there thinking I should feel happy, I have nothing to do, and instead I just felt this awful emptiness. Like, what is all of this for? And I kind of laughed it off at the time. But looking back, I think that was the moment something changed.

Dr: That Sunday sounds like it stayed with you, even if you tried to set it aside. It takes a lot of self-awareness to trace something back like that. Can I ask — is there anyone in your life right now, besides your sister, that you feel you can be honest with about how you're really doing?

Client: No. Not really. I mean I have friends but I perform being okay around them. I laugh at the right times, I ask about their lives. It's easier than explaining. Because if I start explaining I'm worried I won't be able to stop, or that I'll say it out loud and it'll become more real, or... I don't know. That they won't know what to do with it and then I'll feel worse.

Dr: That makes a lot of sense — protecting both yourself and them. And yet here you are, saying it out loud to me. How does that feel, right now, in this moment?

Client: *(exhales slowly)* Scary. But also kind of... like I've been holding my breath for a really long time and I just took a small one.

---

*[End of transcript — Session continues]*
English.sample_3:
# Therapy Session Transcript
**Session #4 | Client: M.R. | Clinician: Dr. Patel**

---

Dr: So, it's good to see you again. How have things been since we last spoke?

Client: Honestly? Not great. I mean, I've had moments where I thought, okay, maybe this week will be different. But then it just... it wasn't.

Dr: Can you tell me more about that? What did those moments look like?

Client: Like, Tuesday morning I woke up and the sun was coming through the window, and for maybe thirty seconds I felt normal. Like myself. And then I remembered everything waiting for me and it just... collapsed. That's the only word I have for it. It collapsed.

Dr: That sounds really heavy. When you say everything waiting for you — what comes to mind first?

Client: Work, mostly. I have this project that's been hanging over me for three weeks now. My manager keeps moving the deadline but not actually reducing what I have to do, so it's like... false relief, you know? And then there's my sister. We haven't really talked since the thing at my mom's birthday. And I don't even know how to start that conversation.

Dr: It sounds like you're carrying a lot from different directions at once. How is that sitting with you physically? In your body?

Client: I don't sleep. I mean, I sleep but I don't really sleep. I'll be in bed by eleven, which is good, right? But I'm awake at two, three in the morning just... running through things. Like my brain won't stop filing papers. And then I'm exhausted all day and I can't focus, and then I feel like I'm failing at work, and then I can't sleep because I'm worried about failing at work. It's this loop.

Dr: That loop sounds exhausting. And I want to come back to that. But first — you mentioned your sister. You said you don't know how to start that conversation. What's holding you back there?

Client: I'm scared, I think. She said something at my mom's party that... it was in front of everyone, and it was about my choices, about my job, about the fact that I'm not married yet, and I just stood there. I didn't say anything. And I've been angry about it ever since, but I'm also — I feel guilty for being angry? Because she's my sister and she probably didn't mean it as badly as it landed.

Dr: So there's anger, but also guilt sitting right beside it.

Client: Yeah. And I miss her. Which makes it worse, honestly. If I didn't care, it would be easier to just let it go. But I do care, so I'm just stuck.

Dr: When you imagine reaching out to her, what do you picture happening?

Client: Either she apologizes and we cry and it's fine. Or she gets defensive and it becomes this whole thing and my mom gets involved and suddenly I'm the problem. I've played it out a hundred times. Both versions.

Dr: And which one feels more likely to you, in your gut?

Client: The second one. Which is probably not fair to her. But that's where my head goes.

Dr: I notice you're quick to question your own instincts there. Does that happen often — second-guessing how you read situations?

Client: All the time. It's like I don't trust myself anymore. I used to be decisive. I used to make a decision and just move. Now I'll spend forty minutes deciding if I should respond to an email or wait until tomorrow. It's pathetic, honestly.

Dr: I'd gently push back on that word — pathetic. What do you think is underneath that indecision?

Client: Fear, I guess. Fear of getting it wrong. Like, somewhere along the way I started believing that if I make a mistake, it means something is fundamentally wrong with me. Not just that I made a mistake, but that I am a mistake.

Dr: That's a really significant thing to name. How long have you been carrying that belief?

Client: I don't know. Maybe always, in some form. But it got louder about a year ago. Around when my dad got sick. Everything felt like it was shifting and I had no control, and I think I started holding everything tighter because of that. Like if I just did everything perfectly, nothing else bad would happen.

Dr: And of course, that's not how life works. Which must make it feel even more defeating when things slip.

Client: Exactly. And they always slip. So now I'm just waiting for the next thing. I wake up and there's this low hum of dread, like something is about to go wrong. Even on good days.

Dr: That constant state of anticipation sounds really draining. Is there anything right now — even something small — that feels like solid ground? Something that doesn't feel like it's shifting?

Client: My dog, maybe. That sounds ridiculous.

Dr: It doesn't sound ridiculous at all.

Client: When I come home and she's just happy to see me, no matter what kind of day I had... it's the one moment where I don't feel like I have to explain myself or perform or get it right. She just doesn't care. And that's... yeah. That helps.

Dr: That matters. We should talk more about that — what it feels like to exist in a space without that pressure. I think there's something important there. Are you okay to continue exploring that next week?

Client: Yeah. I think so. I actually feel a little lighter saying it out loud.

Dr: Good. That's what this space is for.

---

*[End of session transcript — 47 minutes elapsed]*

Hindi.sample_1:
**Therapy Session Transcript**
*Language: Hindi | Estimated Duration: 6–7 minutes*

---

Dr: Namaste. Aaj aap kaisa feel kar rahe hain? Kuch khaas jo aap share karna chahein?

Client: Namaste, Doctor sahab. Main... honestly bolun toh, bahut thaka hua hun. Andar se. Jaise koi battery hai jo kabhi charge hi nahi hoti.

Dr: Hmm. Yeh jo thakan aap bol rahe hain — kya yeh sirf shareerik hai, ya kuch aur bhi chal raha hai mann mein?

Client: Dono. Par... mann mein zyada hai. Subah uthta hun toh pehla khayal yahi aata hai — "aaj bhi wohi sab karna padega." Koi excitement nahi. Koi... koi cheez nahi jiske liye uthne ka mann kare.

Dr: Yeh jo aap keh rahe hain — "wohi sab" — kya aap thoda aur bata sakte hain? Woh "sab" kya hai?

Client: Office. Meetings. Log. Mera manager hai — woh roz nayi expectations laata hai. Aur main... main koshish karta hun poori karne ki, lekin kabhi bhi enough nahi lagta. Ek kaam khatam nahi hota, doosra aa jaata hai. Aur ghar aakar bhi wahi sochta rehta hun. Dinner table pe bhi phone haath mein rehta hai. Biwi kehti hai kuch, main sunta hun lekin... wahan hota nahi.

Dr: Yeh jo aapne kaha — "wahan hota nahi" — yeh bahut meaningful baat hai. Aisa kab se ho raha hai? Koi ek waqt yaad hai jab yeh shuru hua?

Client: *(pause)* Shayad... chhe mahine pehle? Jab promotion nahi mili. Main sochta tha — do saal ki mehnat, raat ko jaagna, sab kuch — aur phir bola gaya ki "agle saal dekhenge." Agle saal. Jaise main koi project hun jo pending mein daal diya.

Dr: Woh sunkar kaisa laga tha? Woh pal jab woh baat suni?

Client: *(lamba pause)* Kuch nahi laga. Yahi toh problem hai. Rona bhi nahi aaya. Gussa bhi nahi aaya. Bas ek... khaalipan tha. Ghar aaya, sofa pe baitha, ek ghante tak kuch nahi kiya. Biwi ne poochha kya hua, main ne bola "kuch nahi." Woh samjh gayi thi, par main khud nahi samjha kya hua mujhe.

Dr: Aur abhi — woh khaalipan aata hai kabhi kabhi, ya zyada rehta hai?

Client: Zyada. Pehle kabhi kabhi aata tha. Ab toh... subah se raat tak ek darr rehta hai. Jaise kuch galat hone waala hai. Koi specific cheez nahi, bas ek... ek bhaari sa ehsaas. Chhati pe.

Dr: Yeh darr — kya aap ise thoda describe kar sakte hain? Woh kaisa feel hota hai physically?

Client: Jaise... koi cheez dabaaye baiThi ho. Kabhi kabhi saansein bhi choti ho jaati hain. Office mein ek baar meeting mein aisa hua — main baat karne waala tha, aur achanak laga ki bolna nahi aayega. Dil bahut tez dhad dhad karne laga. Kisi ne notice nahi kiya, par main andar se kaanp raha tha.

Dr: Aapne bahut himmat se yeh share kiya. Aur neend? Raat ko kaisa hota hai?

Client: Neend... *(halki hansi)* ...woh toh mehman ban gayi hai. Aati hai kabhi kabhi, zyada nahi rukti. Sau jaata hun phir do baje uthta hun. Phir wohi sochna shuru — kaam, paisa, bache ka school, EMI... sab ek saath. Aur phir subah uthna padta hai thaka hua.

Dr: Bache ka school aapne mention kiya. Ghar mein kya chal raha hai? Unke saath kaisa time guzar raha hai?

Client: *(ruk jaata hai)* Woh... woh mere paas aata hai khelne ke liye. Aur main koshish karta hun. Par kabhi kabhi main screen pe hota hun aur woh mujhe hilata rehta hai. Ek din usne kaha — "Papa, aap hamesha busy rehte ho." Woh paanch saal ka hai. Paanch saal ka bachcha yeh bol raha hai mujhe. *(awaaz thodi bhaari hoti hai)*

Dr: Yeh sunke andar kya hua? Woh moment mein?

Client: Bahut dard hua. Pehli baar bahut dard hua. Promotion wali baat pe nahi roya, par us raat — jab woh so gaya — main bathroom mein gaya aur... *(rukta hai)* ...thoda roya. Akele. Kyunki main jaanta hun main woh baap nahi ban pa raha jo banna chahta hun.

Dr: Yeh bahut important hai jo aapne abhi bataya. Yeh dard — yeh toot-ta hua aur phir bhi khade rehne ki koshish — yeh dikha raha hai ki aap kaafi kuch akele uthaa rahe hain. Kya aapne kabhi apni biwi se, ya kisi aur se, yeh sab khulke bola hai?

Client: Nahi. Unhe tension nahi dena. Woh pehle se bahut handle karti hain. Main sochta hun ki main hi strong rehun. Par... shayad main galat soch raha hun. Mujhe khud nahi pata ki strong rehna aur chup rehna — yeh ek cheez hai ya alag alag.

Dr: Yeh sawaal jo aapne khud se poochha — yeh bahut gehra sawaal hai. Aur aaj yahan aana, yeh sab bolna — yeh bhi ek tarah ki himmat hai. Hum aage milke samjhenge ki yeh jo bojh hai — isko thoda halka kaise kiya jaaye. Kaisa lagega?

Client: *(dheere se)* Achha lagega. Bahut achha lagega.

---

*[Session continues...]*
Hindi.sample_2:
# थेरेपी सत्र — प्रतिलेख

---

**Dr:** नमस्ते, आइए बैठिए। कैसा महसूस हो रहा है आज आपको?

**Client:** नमस्ते... हाँ, ठीक हूँ। मतलब... ठीक तो नहीं हूँ, इसीलिए आया हूँ। बस थोड़ा... नहीं पता, सब कुछ उलझा-उलझा सा लग रहा है इन दिनों।

---

**Dr:** उलझा-उलझा... यह सुनकर लगा कि काफी कुछ चल रहा है मन में। क्या आप थोड़ा और बता सकते हैं — कब से यह महसूस हो रहा है?

**Client:** शायद... दो-तीन महीने से। पहले मैंने सोचा कि काम का बोझ है, थोड़ा आराम मिलेगा तो ठीक हो जाएगा। लेकिन वो आराम आया ही नहीं। और अब तो... रात को भी नींद नहीं आती ठीक से। लेटता हूँ तो दिमाग बंद नहीं होता।

---

**Dr:** रात को नींद नहीं आती — यह बहुत थका देने वाला होता है। जब आप लेटते हैं और दिमाग बंद नहीं होता, तो उस वक्त क्या चलता रहता है मन में?

**Client:** बस... काम की चिंता। यह प्रोजेक्ट पूरा होगा कि नहीं, मेरा मैनेजर क्या सोचता होगा मेरे बारे में, अगले महीने का टारगेट... और फिर उसके बाद घर की बातें आ जाती हैं। पापा की तबियत, छोटी बहन की शादी, EMI... एक के बाद एक। जैसे एक लंबी queue हो और उसका कोई अंत नहीं।

---

**Dr:** एक लंबी queue जिसका अंत नहीं — यह बहुत सटीक कहा आपने। इतनी सारी जिम्मेदारियाँ एक साथ... आप यह सब किससे शेयर करते हैं? कोई है जिससे बात होती है?

**Client:** *(थोड़ी देर चुप रहते हैं)* ...नहीं, ज़्यादा नहीं। दोस्त हैं, लेकिन सबकी अपनी-अपनी life है। और घर में बताऊँगा तो माँ परेशान हो जाएंगी। पापा वैसे ही बीमार हैं। तो लगता है... बस खुद ही handle करो। लेकिन अब खुद भी नहीं हो रहा।

---

**Dr:** यह सुनकर मुझे लगा कि आप काफी अकेले carry कर रहे हैं यह सब। जब आप कहते हैं "अब खुद भी नहीं हो रहा" — तो वो कैसा लगता है? उस moment में आप क्या feel करते हैं?

**Client:** एक तरह की... थकान। लेकिन नींद वाली नहीं। अंदर से कुछ खाली-खाली सा। पिछले हफ्ते ऑफिस में था, सब हँस रहे थे किसी बात पर, और मैं वहाँ बैठा था लेकिन जैसे... था ही नहीं। शरीर था, लेकिन मैं कहीं और था। यह डरा गया मुझे।

---

**Dr:** वो पल जब आप वहाँ थे लेकिन "थे नहीं" — इसने आपको डराया, यह जानकर अच्छा लगा कि आपने उसे notice किया। क्या ऐसा अक्सर होता है, यह disconnected महसूस करना?

**Client:** हाँ... हाँ, होता है। काम के बीच में अचानक लगता है जैसे सब बेकार है। मेहनत करो, टारगेट पूरा करो, फिर अगला टारगेट। इसका क्या मतलब है? यह सवाल बहुत आता है। और फिर मैं खुद को डाँटता हूँ — "अरे, इतने लोग इससे भी मुश्किल हालात में हैं, तू क्यों रो रहा है।"

---

**Dr:** आप खुद को डाँटते हैं — यह बहुत ज़रूरी बात है जो आपने कही। क्या आपको लगता है कि आप जो महसूस कर रहे हैं, उसके लिए आपको खुद को justify करना पड़ता है?

**Client:** *(धीरे से)* हाँ। हमेशा। जैसे मेरी तकलीफ "काफी बड़ी" नहीं है। कोई बड़ी accident नहीं हुई, कोई बड़ा नुकसान नहीं हुआ। तो फिर यह क्यों है यह सब? इसीलिए किसी से बोला नहीं। सोचा लोग क्या सोचेंगे — "इतनी सी बात में रो रहा है।"

---

**Dr:** आपकी तकलीफ को किसी बड़े कारण की ज़रूरत नहीं होती — वो real है, बस इसलिए कि आप उसे महसूस कर रहे हैं। यह बात आज यहाँ रखना — इसमें हिम्मत लगी। आगे की बात करें — भविष्य के बारे में, आने वाले समय के बारे में आप कैसा सोचते हैं? कोई उम्मीद दिखती है?

**Client:** *(लंबी साँस लेते हैं)* कभी-कभी दिखती है। जैसे... सोचता हूँ कि अगर यह नौकरी छोड़ दूँ तो शायद ठीक होऊँगा। फिर सोचता हूँ — छोड़ूँगा तो घर कैसे चलेगा। तो एक loop है। बाहर निकलने का रास्ता दिखता ही नहीं। बस... चलते रहो, यही समझ आता है। लेकिन कब तक चलूँगा, यह नहीं पता।

---

**Dr:** "कब तक चलूँगा" — यह सवाल बहुत भारी है। और आज आपने इसे यहाँ लाने का फैसला किया — यह छोटा कदम नहीं है। अगले कुछ सत्रों में हम इस loop को साथ मिलकर समझने की कोशिश करेंगे — धीरे-धीरे। आपको अकेले नहीं करना यह।

**Client:** *(आँखें थोड़ी नम)* ठीक है... शुक्रिया। बहुत दिनों बाद किसी से बोला हूँ यह सब।

**थेरेपी सत्र — प्रतिलिपि**
*दिनांक: गोपनीय | सत्र संख्या: 3*

---

Dr: नमस्ते, आइए बैठिए। कैसा महसूस हो रहा है आज आपको?

Client: नमस्ते... हाँ, बस... ठीक हूँ। मतलब, ऐसे तो ठीक हूँ, लेकिन... नहीं पता। थोड़ा भारीपन सा है अंदर से।

Dr: भारीपन... वो थोड़ा और बताइए। किस तरह का भारीपन?

Client: जैसे... जैसे सुबह उठता हूँ तो लगता है कि उठना ही नहीं है। बिस्तर में पड़े रहो। काम पर जाने का मन नहीं करता। लेकिन जाना पड़ता है, तो जाता हूँ। बस... खींचकर खुद को।

Dr: खींचकर खुद को... यह बहुत थका देने वाला होता है। पिछले कुछ हफ्तों से यह हो रहा है, या पहले से भी था?

Client: नहीं, पहले ऐसा नहीं था। शायद... दो-तीन महीने से। जब से वो प्रोजेक्ट आया है ऑफिस में। बड़ा वाला। मेरे manager ने मुझे lead बना दिया, जो कि... अच्छी बात है, मुझे पता है। लेकिन... मुझे नहीं पता मैं कर पाऊँगा या नहीं।

Dr: तो एक तरफ recognition है, और दूसरी तरफ डर भी है। यह डर कैसा लगता है? कहाँ महसूस होता है?

Client: *(रुककर)* यहाँ... सीने में। जैसे कुछ दबा हुआ है हमेशा। और रात को... रात को सोते वक्त बहुत ज़्यादा होता है। दिमाग बंद ही नहीं होता। सोचता रहता हूँ — क्या होगा अगर कुछ गलत हो गया? टीम क्या सोचेगी? Boss क्या सोचेगा?

Dr: नींद कैसी है आपकी? ठीक से हो पाती है?

Client: नहीं। मतलब, पड़ता तो हूँ जल्दी, लेकिन... घंटे भर तक आँखें खुली रहती हैं। और कभी-कभी बीच रात में उठ जाता हूँ। फिर वही सोचने लगता हूँ। कल की meeting, deadline, कि अगर कुछ miss हो गया तो...

Dr: यह जो रात को विचार आते हैं — क्या वो सिर्फ काम के बारे में होते हैं, या कुछ और भी?

Client: *(थोड़ी देर चुप रहते हैं)* ...कभी-कभी घर के बारे में भी। माँ की तबियत ठीक नहीं रहती। वो गाँव में हैं। मैं यहाँ अकेला हूँ। लगता है कि... सब कुछ एक साथ आ गया है। माँ को देखने जाना चाहिए, लेकिन छुट्टी नहीं मिलती। और अगर गया तो project पीछे रह जाएगा।

Dr: यह बहुत मुश्किल स्थिति है — एक बेटे की जिम्मेदारी, और एक professional की जिम्मेदारी — दोनों एक साथ। आप किससे बात करते हैं इन सब चीज़ों के बारे में?

Client: किससे करूँ? *(हल्की हँसी, लेकिन आँखें थोड़ी भर आती हैं)* ऑफिस में तो सब competitive हैं। दोस्त हैं, लेकिन उनकी भी अपनी life है। और घर फोन करो तो माँ को tension हो जाती है। तो... बस खुद ही रखता हूँ।

Dr: खुद ही रखते हैं... यह बोझ कितना भारी हो जाता है?

Client: बहुत। *(रुकते हैं, गला भर आता है)* कभी-कभी लगता है कि थक गया हूँ। सच में। जैसे अंदर से कोई चीज़ खाली हो रही है। पहले जो चीज़ें अच्छी लगती थीं — cricket देखना, दोस्तों के साथ बाहर जाना — वो भी अब नहीं लगतीं।

Dr: जो चीज़ें पहले खुशी देती थीं, वो अब नहीं देतीं — यह सुनकर मुझे आपकी बहुत चिंता होती है। क्या कभी ऐसा लगा है कि बस... सब छोड़ दें? या खुद को नुकसान पहुँचाने के कोई विचार आए हैं?

Client: *(सोचते हुए)* नहीं... नहीं, ऐसा नहीं। बस थकान है। भागना चाहता हूँ — जैसे कहीं चला जाऊँ, पहाड़ों में, जहाँ कोई phone नहीं, कोई deadline नहीं। बस वो वाला भागना।

Dr: ठीक है, यह समझ आया। वो एक राहत की तलाश है। यह बताइए — जब आप यहाँ आते हैं, इन बातों को यहाँ रखते हैं — थोड़ा हल्का लगता है?

Client: हाँ... थोड़ा। यहाँ कम से कम बोल पाता हूँ। बाहर तो बस mask लगाए रहो — "हाँ, सब ठीक है, हाँ, handle हो जाएगा।" *(धीमे से)* लेकिन कितना handle करूँ?

Dr: यही सवाल बहुत ज़रूरी है — कितना handle करूँ। आज आपने जो share किया, वो बहुत honest था। अगले हफ्ते हम इस पर थोड़ा काम करेंगे — कि यह बोझ थोड़ा बाँटा कैसे जाए। लेकिन अभी, इस वक्त — आप कैसा महसूस कर रहे हैं?

Client: *(एक लंबी साँस)* थोड़ा... थोड़ा हल्का। थोड़ा सा।

**थेरपी संवाद — मराठी**

*(एक शांत, सुरक्षित थेरपी रूममध्ये)*

---

Dr: नमस्कार. या. बसा आरामात. आज कसं वाटतंय तुम्हाला?

Client: नमस्कार, डॉक्टर. बसतो... हं. कसं वाटतंय म्हणजे... खरं सांगायचं तर खूप दमलेलो आहे. आतून. शरीराने नाही, म्हणजे शरीरानेही आहेच, पण आतून जास्त.

Dr: आतून दमलेलो आहात... हे ऐकून मला समजतंय. हे थकणं कधापासून जाणवतंय तुम्हाला?

Client: साधारण तीन-चार महिन्यांपासून असेल. पण गेल्या महिन्यात खूपच वाढलंय. म्हणजे सकाळी उठतो तेव्हाच जड वाटतं. जणू रात्री झोपलोच नाही असं. आणि खरंच झोप नीट होत नाही हल्ली. रात्री डोळे मिटले की विचारच येतात, थांबतच नाहीत.

Dr: रात्री विचार येतात... कसले विचार असतात ते? थोडं सांगता का?

Client: हं... ऑफिसचं खूप असतं. म्हणजे, आमच्या टीममध्ये नवीन प्रोजेक्ट आलाय, आणि माझ्यावर खूप जबाबदारी आहे. बॉस म्हणतात तू हॅन्डल कर, तुलाच जमेल. पण मला आतून भीती वाटते की... की मी चुकलो तर? की मी नाही जमवू शकलो तर? मग रात्री हे सगळं डोक्यात फिरत राहतं. डेडलाइन, मीटिंग, कोणाला काय वाटेल...

Dr: हे ऐकताना जाणवतंय की तुम्हाला खूप दडपण आहे या जबाबदारीचं. आणि चुकण्याची भीती... ही भीती नक्की कुठून येते असं वाटतं तुम्हाला?

Client: *(थोडा थांबतो)* हे... हे मला नीट सांगता येत नाही. पण लहानपणापासूनच असं आहे जरा. घरात बाबा खूप स्ट्रिक्ट होते. चांगलं केलं तरी म्हणायचे 'आणखी चांगलं करता आलं असतं.' त्यामुळे कायम असं वाटायचं की मी कमी पडतोय. आणि आता ऑफिसमध्ये पण तेच... म्हणजे माझे बॉस तसे बोलत नाहीत, पण मी स्वतःच स्वतःला तसं म्हणतो बहुतेक.

Dr: हे खूप महत्त्वाचं बोललात. म्हणजे बाहेरून कोणी नाही सांगत, पण आतला आवाज खूप कठोर आहे स्वतःबद्दल. हे जेव्हा होतं, तेव्हा तुमच्या मनात नक्की काय येतं स्वतःबद्दल?

Client: *(हळूच)* की मी... की मी पुरेसा नाही. की बाकी सगळे इतके चांगले करतात, मग मी का नाही? माझ्या टीममधला अमित आहे, तो इतक्या सहज सगळं करतो. कधी काळजी नाही, कधी टेन्शन नाही. मला वाटतं मी त्याच्यासारखा का नाही? मग आणखी वाईट वाटतं स्वतःबद्दल.

Dr: दुसऱ्यांशी स्वतःची तुलना करताना खूप एकटं वाटतं का कधी कधी?

Client: हो... हो, खूप. *(श्वास घेतो)* घरी बायकोला सांगतो थोडं, पण ती म्हणते 'जास्त विचार करतोस तू.' ती चुकीचं नाही म्हणत, पण मग बोलणंच थांबवतो. कारण वाटतं, माझ्या मनातलं कोणाला समजत नाही. मग एकट्यानेच बसतो. आणि जास्तच विचार होतात.

Dr: म्हणजे जेव्हा बोलायला जाता, तेव्हा समजलं नाही असं वाटतं, आणि मग आणखी एकटं वाटतं. हे किती कठीण असेल... या सगळ्यात तुम्हाला स्वतःसाठी काही वेळ मिळतो का? काहीतरी ज्याने बरं वाटतं असं?

Client: *(विचार करतो)* पूर्वी क्रिकेट खेळायचो. रोज संध्याकाळी मित्रांसोबत. पण गेल्या वर्षभरात ते बंद झालं. कोणाला वेळ नाही, मलाही नाही. पूर्वी वाचन पण करायचो... आता पुस्तक उघडलं तरी डोळे वाचतात पण मन कुठेतरी दुसरीकडेच असतं. ऑफिसमध्येच अडकलेलं.

Dr: हे ऐकताना जाणवतं की जे तुम्हाला आनंद द्यायचं ते हळूहळू दूर गेलंय. आणि हे होताना तुम्हाला कसं वाटत होतं?

Client: *(हळू आवाजात)* रिकामं. खूप रिकामं वाटलं. म्हणजे सगळं आहे — नोकरी आहे, घर आहे, बायको आहे, सगळं ठीक आहे बाहेरून. पण आतून... आतून काहीतरी हरवलंय असं वाटतं. कधी कधी विचार येतो की हे असंच राहणार का? म्हणजे पुढे काय होणार? हे थांबेल का कधी?

Dr: हा प्रश्न — 'हे थांबेल का?' — हा विचार येतो तेव्हा मनात काय असतं? म्हणजे पुढे काय दिसतं तुम्हाला?

Client: *(थांबतो, डोळे ओले होतात)* माहीत नाही. म्हणजे... मला कोणाला दुखवायचं नाही, तसं नाही. पण थकलोय इतका की कधी कधी वाटतं हे सगळं थांबलं तर? हे ओझं उतरलं तर? पण मग बायकोचा, मुलाचा विचार येतो आणि मागे हटतो. *(हळूच)* हे बोललो आज पहिल्यांदाच कोणाशी.

Dr: हे बोलणं किती धाडसाचं आहे. तुम्ही आज जे सांगितलं ते खूप महत्त्वाचं आहे. तुम्ही एकटे नाही आहात आता. आपण यावर एकत्र काम करणार आहोत, हळूहळू. ठीक आहे?

Client: *(हळूच मान डोलावतो)* हो... हो, ठीक आहे. थँक्यू, डॉक्टर.

# थेरपी संवाद — मराठी

---

**Dr:** नमस्कार. या आठवड्यात कसं वाटतंय तुम्हाला? काय चाललंय मनात?

---

**Dr:** ठीक आहे, घाई नाही. जिथून सहज वाटेल तिथून सांगा. हे जडपण... कधीपासून जाणवतंय तुम्हाला?

**Client:** साधारण दोन-अडीच महिन्यांपासून. ऑफिसमध्ये नवीन प्रोजेक्ट आलं — खूप मोठं आहे, आणि माझ्यावरच जास्त जबाबदारी टाकली आहे. बॉसने थेट सांगितलं की हे यशस्वी झालं नाही तर... तर पुढचं काय होईल ते माहीत नाही असं. त्यांनी नक्की काय म्हणायचं होतं ते सांगितलं नाही, पण मला जाणवलं... म्हणजे, मला वाटलं की माझी नोकरी धोक्यात आहे. तेव्हापासून रात्री झोप येत नाही नीट.

---

**Dr:** हे ऐकून जाणवतं की तुमच्यावर खूप दबाव आहे. झोप येत नाही म्हणजे... रात्री नक्की काय होतं? मन कुठे जातं?

**Client:** हं... झोपायला जातो, डोळे मिटतो, पण डोक्यात विचारांचं एक चक्रच सुरू होतं. उद्या हे काम होईल का, presentation चांगली होईल का, टीमने माझ्यावर विश्वास ठेवला आहे का... आणि मग एकदम खूप दूरचे विचार येतात. म्हणजे नोकरी गेली तर घराचं काय, आईचं काय, EMI चं काय... एका विचाराचा धागा दुसऱ्याला जोडतो आणि मग रात्र संपते. सकाळी उठतो तेव्हा आणखी थकलेलो असतो.

---

**Dr:** हे खूप कठीण आहे. एकट्याने हे सगळं सहन करणं... घरी कोणाशी बोलता का याबद्दल? आई किंवा इतर कोणी?

**Client:** *(थांबतो)* नाही... म्हणजे, आईला सांगायची इच्छा असते, पण ती आधीच खूप काळजीत असते. तिची तब्येत बरी नसते अधूनमधून, तिला आणखी ताण नको द्यायला. आणि बाबा... बाबा म्हणतात की "आमच्या वेळी असलं नव्हतं, कामाचा ताण असतोच, सहन करायचं असतं." त्यांना समजावून सांगणं कठीण वाटतं. मित्र आहेत, पण ते पण त्यांच्या आयुष्यात बिझी आहेत. मग वाटतं की बोलून काय उपयोग...

---

**Dr:** "बोलून काय उपयोग" — हे वाक्य मनात येतं तेव्हा आतमध्ये कसं वाटतं? एकटेपणाची भावना येते का?

**Client:** *(हळूच)* हो. खूप एकटेपणा वाटतो. हे मला आधी नव्हतं जाणवत इतकं. पण आता... कधी कधी ऑफिसमध्ये सगळे बोलत असतात, हसत असतात, आणि मी तिथे असतो पण आतून कुठे तरी दुसरीकडेच असतो. जसं काच आहे माझ्यामध्ये आणि बाकी सगळ्यांमध्ये. हे सांगायला विचित्र वाटतं, पण तसंच होतं.

---

**Dr:** नाही, विचित्र नाही अजिबात. तुम्ही खूप स्पष्टपणे सांगत आहात — ती "काचेची" भावना — ती खूप महत्त्वाची आहे. आणि स्वतःबद्दल काय वाटतं या सगळ्यात? स्वतःला कसं पाहता तुम्ही?

**Client:** *(दीर्घ श्वास)* हे... हे विचारलंत तर... मला वाटतं मी कमी पडतोय. सगळ्यांना वाटतं मी capable आहे, पण मला आतून वाटतं की मी एक दिवस सगळ्यांना निराश करणार. हे प्रोजेक्ट, आई, सगळे... मी खरंच त्यांच्या अपेक्षांएवढा आहे का, हा प्रश्न सतत येतो. *(थांबतो)* आणि उत्तर येत नाही.

---

**Dr:** तुम्ही जे सांगितलं ते ऐकून मला जाणवतं की तुम्ही स्वतःवर खूप कठोर आहात. हे "मी कमी पडतो" हे विचार — ते किती वेळ असतात दिवसात?

**Client:** खूप वेळ. म्हणजे... सकाळी उठल्यापासून. आरशात पाहतो, तेव्हा पण येतात. ऑफिसला जाताना गाडीत पण. कधी कधी काम करताना अचानक मध्येच येतात आणि मग concentration जातं, मग परत वाटतं की बघ, नीट काम पण करता येत नाही. एक चक्र आहे जणू.

---

**Dr:** हे चक्र खूप थकवणारं आहे, हे मला समजतं. तुम्ही आज इथे आलात, बोललात — हे धाडस आहे. आता मला सांगा — या सगळ्यात कधी असा विचार आला का की... पुढे जाणंच नको, किंवा सगळ्यातून निघून जावंसं वाटतं?

**Client:** *(शांत राहतो काही क्षण)* निघून जाणं... म्हणजे कुठे तरी एकट्याने जाऊन बसावंसं वाटतं, कोणाला भेटू नये असं वाटतं — तसं होतं. पण... स्वतःला दुखवणं किंवा तसलं — नाही, तसं नाही. फक्त सगळं थांबवायचंय थोडा वेळ. एक pause हवाय.

---

**Dr:** हे ऐकून बरं वाटलं की तुम्ही स्पष्ट सांगितलं. "Pause हवाय" — ही भावना खूप खरी आहे. आपण पुढे एकत्र काम करू यावर. आज तुम्ही जे शेअर केलं — ते खूप मौल्यवान आहे. तुम्हाला एकट्याने हे सहन करायची गरज नाही, ठीक आहे?

**Client:** *(हळूच)* हो... आज बोललो तर थोडं हलकं वाटतंय. खूप दिवसांनी कोणाशी तरी नीट बोललो. थँक्यू, डॉक्टर.

**Dr:** तुम्ही एकटे नाही आहात — हे आपण एकत्र काम करत शोधणार आहोत. आजचं हे संभाषण हा एक महत्त्वाचा पहिला पाऊल आहे. पुढच्या वेळी आपण यावर अजून खोलवर बोलू. तुम्ही आलात, हे खूप महत्त्वाचं आहे.

# Therapy Session Transcript

**Session Date:** March 15, 2024
**Duration:** Approximately 7 minutes

---

Dr: Good morning. How are you doing today? What brings you in?

Client: I'm... I don't know. I'm okay, I guess. Well, not really okay. My wife actually suggested I come. She said I've been different lately, and I wasn't sure if she was right, but then I realized I haven't been sleeping well for like... two months now? Maybe longer. I'm not even sure anymore.

Dr: Two months is a significant stretch. Can you tell me more about what the sleep has been like? What's happening when you try to sleep?

Client: It's weird because I'm exhausted during the day. Like, bone-tired. But when I get into bed, my mind just... won't stop. I lie there and think about work stuff, like projects that aren't even due yet, or I'll think about something I said to my boss three weeks ago and suddenly I'm embarrassed all over again. And then it's 2 AM and I'm still awake.

Dr: And when you're lying there at 2 AM with all these thoughts, what's that experience like emotionally? How does it feel?

Client: *long pause* Frustrated, I guess. And anxious? Like I know I need to sleep because I have to work the next day, and that knowledge itself makes me more awake. It's like a trap. I get more stressed about not sleeping, which keeps me awake more. My wife says I should just turn off my phone and meditate or something, but...

Dr: But what?

Client: But I can't seem to do it. I tell myself I'll put the phone down, and then fifteen minutes later I'm checking my work email again. I don't even realize I'm doing it sometimes. It's automatic.

Dr: So there's this pull toward the work stuff, even when you know it's not helping you sleep. When you think about work, what specifically worries you? Is there something particular happening at work right now?

Client: *shifts in seat* Yeah, actually. We had some restructuring about four months ago, and I wasn't sure if my position was secure. It worked out fine—I'm still here, got a small raise actually—but I think that did something to me. Like, even though it's resolved, I can't shake this feeling that something's going to go wrong. That I need to be on top of everything, you know? Respond to emails immediately, make sure every project is perfect.

Dr: It sounds like even though the threat passed, your nervous system is still in alert mode. Like it learned to be vigilant, and it hasn't quite gotten the memo that things stabilized.

Client: *nods slowly* Yeah, that's... that's actually exactly what it feels like. It's exhausting. And I know it doesn't make sense because I got the raise, so clearly they value me. But my brain isn't believing that.

Dr: What does the exhaustion feel like in your body, aside from just the lack of sleep? Are you noticing anything else?

Client: I get these headaches. Tension headaches, the doctor said. They usually start in the afternoon and just build throughout the day. And my shoulders... *touches shoulders* ...they're always tight. Even when I'm sitting doing nothing, I notice I'm clenching everything. My wife has to literally tell me to relax my face because apparently I'm frowning without knowing it.

Dr: And how is that affecting your relationship with your wife? You mentioned she's the one who suggested you come.

Client: *quieter voice* She's frustrated. I can tell. She'll ask me about my day and I'll give her one-word answers. Or I'll snap at her about something small and then immediately feel terrible. Like, she'll ask if I want dinner and I'll react like she's attacking me. And then I have to apologize because she didn't do anything wrong.

Dr: How often is that happening?

Client: Pretty regularly now. Maybe three or four times a week? And she's started going to bed before me because she said she can't sleep when I'm tossing and turning. So now we're not even sleeping together, which just makes me feel worse because now I'm affecting her sleep too.

Dr: That sounds like you're carrying some guilt about that.

Client: *voice cracks slightly* Yeah. A lot, actually. She's been really supportive about everything, and instead of me being supportive back, I'm just... withdrawing. And then I feel bad about withdrawing, which makes me more anxious, which makes me sleep worse. It's all connected.

Dr: These things often are. What you're describing—the sleep disruption, the racing thoughts at night, the physical tension, the irritability with someone you care about, the difficulty shaking worry even after the stressor is gone—these all tend to feed each other.

Client: So what does that mean? Is this anxiety? I always thought anxiety was like panic attacks and stuff. I don't have panic attacks.

Dr: Anxiety can look very different from person to person. Some people experience it as panic, yes, but many experience it more like what you're describing—a persistent sense of threat, difficulty sleeping, physical tension, trouble focusing. It's like your alarm system got turned up too high.

Client: *long pause* So can it get better? Or is this just who I am now?

Dr: That's a really important question, and I want to be honest with you—yes, this can absolutely get better. But it usually requires some intentional work. I'd like to understand more about your history and what coping strategies you've tried so far. But first, I'm curious: before the restructuring four months ago, how were things? How was your sleep, your stress level?

Client: *nods thoughtfully* It was normal. I mean, work was still work, but I wasn't lying awake at night. I had energy. I wasn't snapping at my wife. I felt okay.

Dr: So you have a reference point for what "okay" feels like for you.

Client: I do. And I miss it.

Dr: That's something we can work toward getting back to. Let's continue exploring this together.

---

**[End of Session Excerpt]**

# Therapy Session Transcript

**Session Date:** March 14, 2024
**Duration:** Approximately 7 minutes

---

Dr: Good morning. Come in, have a seat. How are you doing today?

Client: *sits down slowly* I'm... I'm okay, I guess. Or, honestly, I'm not sure. I'm tired. I've been tired for a while now.

Dr: Tired in what way? Can you tell me a bit more about that?

Client: *pauses, looking down at hands* It's not just physical, you know? Like, I can sleep for eight hours and still wake up feeling completely drained. But it's also just... everything feels heavy. Work, talking to people, even things I used to enjoy. I don't know when this started exactly, but I'd say maybe... two or three months?

Dr: Two or three months is a significant stretch of time. What was happening around that period? Was there anything that changed in your life?

Client: *shifts in seat* Well, yeah, actually. My company went through a restructuring in December. I didn't lose my job, but a lot of people did. And suddenly I was doing the work of two people. My manager kept saying it was temporary, but... *trails off* ...it's March now, and nothing's changed. If anything, it's gotten worse.

Dr: That sounds like a lot of pressure. How has that been affecting you day-to-day?

Client: *rubs face* I'm constantly anxious. I check my email first thing in the morning—sometimes before I even get out of bed—just to see if there's some new crisis I need to handle. My heart rate goes up just thinking about opening my laptop. And then at night, even though I'm exhausted, I can't really sleep. My mind just... keeps going. I'll lie there for hours thinking about projects, deadlines, whether I'm doing enough.

Dr: So there's this cycle happening—the anxiety keeps you from sleeping well, and the fatigue makes it harder to cope with the stress. Is that fair?

Client: *nods* Exactly. And then I feel guilty because I know I should be grateful I still have a job. People got laid off. My friend Sarah lost her position, and here I am complaining about having too much work. *voice gets quieter* But I'm struggling, and I feel like I shouldn't be.

Dr: It sounds like you're comparing your experience to others', which is making it harder to acknowledge what you're actually going through. Can I ask—how does that guilt affect you?

Client: *pauses, thinking* It makes everything worse, honestly. Because then I'm not only stressed and tired, I'm also frustrated with myself for being stressed and tired. It's like I'm having two problems at once. And I start to think maybe I'm just weak, or not cut out for this job, or... *voice trails off* ...or maybe there's something actually wrong with me.

Dr: When you say something might be wrong with you—what does that mean?

Client: *looks up* I don't know. Like, maybe I have depression or something? I've never been diagnosed with anything, but my mom has dealt with depression. And sometimes I wonder if... *hesitates* ...if this is more than just job stress. Because even on weekends, when I'm not working, I still feel this heaviness. I canceled plans with friends last weekend because I just couldn't face being around people. I told them I was sick, but I wasn't. I just wanted to be alone.

Dr: Social withdrawal combined with the fatigue and the persistent low mood—those are things worth exploring. Let me ask you this: when you do spend time alone, what does that look like? Are you resting, or are you ruminating?

Client: *laughs without humor* Mostly ruminating, I think. I'll sit on the couch with my phone, scrolling through nothing, thinking about work. Or I'll start a show and realize twenty minutes later I have no idea what I just watched. Sometimes I just stare at the wall. It doesn't feel restful. It feels... *searches for words* ...hollow? Like I'm just existing, not really living.

Dr: That's a really clear description. And I'm hearing that even when you remove yourself from the stressor—the work—the internal experience doesn't shift much. Does that feel accurate?

Client: *nods slowly* Yeah. And that's what scares me a little. Because if it was just the job, I thought maybe taking time off would help. But I don't think it would. I feel like something inside me has... I don't know, broken? Or is breaking?

Dr: I appreciate you sharing that. It took courage to name that fear. *pauses* I want to ask about your sleep more specifically. You mentioned lying awake for hours. When you're lying there, what are the thoughts that come up?

Client: *fidgets with hands* Usually it's work-related at first. Like, replaying conversations from the day, or planning what I need to do tomorrow. But then it spirals into bigger things. Like, what if I make a mistake and get fired anyway? What if I'm not good enough at my job? What if I'm never going to feel normal again? *voice cracks slightly* And that last one—that's the one that really keeps me up. The idea that this is just... who I am now.

Dr: So the worry extends beyond the immediate situation to this larger question about your future and your capacity to feel okay again. That must feel very isolating.

Client: *nods, eyes getting watery* It really does. I haven't told anyone at work how bad it's gotten. And I haven't really talked to my family about it either. My dad would just tell me to work harder or find a new job, and my mom would worry too much. So I'm just... *trails off* ...carrying this alone.

Dr: You're carrying quite a bit. And I think it's important to recognize that what you're describing—the sleep disruption, the fatigue, the withdrawal, the persistent worry, the feeling that something might be fundamentally wrong—these are all things we can work with. They're not character flaws. They're signals that your system is overwhelmed.

Client: *takes a shaky breath* Okay. I... I didn't know if coming here would actually help, but hearing you say that helps a little.

Dr: I'm glad. And I think this is a good place to start. Before our next session, I'd like to learn more about a few other areas of your life. But for now, I want you to know that what you're experiencing is real, and it's worth taking seriously.

---

**End of Session Transcript**
English.sample_3:
# Therapy Session Transcript

**Date:** March 14, 2024
**Duration:** Approximately 7 minutes
**Setting:** Private therapy office

---

Dr: Good morning. How are you doing today? What brings you in?

Client: Yeah, um... *pauses* I'm not really sure where to start, honestly. Things have just been... off lately. I don't know. Maybe for the last few months? I keep telling myself it'll pass, but it's not getting better.

Dr: That sounds frustrating—feeling like something's not quite right but not being able to pinpoint it. Can you tell me a bit more about what "off" feels like for you?

Client: *shifts in seat* It's like... I wake up and there's this heaviness, you know? Even before my feet hit the ground. And I used to love my job. I'm in marketing, and we have decent projects coming up, but I just... I can't seem to get excited about anything anymore. And then I lie awake at night thinking about work, or money, or just... everything. I'll be exhausted the next day, but I still can't sleep the night after. It's this cycle.

Dr: So there's this heaviness in the morning, and then at night your mind is racing with worries—about work, finances, other things. How often would you say you're experiencing these sleep difficulties?

Client: *rubs eyes* Most nights, honestly. Maybe four or five nights a week? I'll fall asleep fine around eleven, but then I'm awake at two or three in the morning, and my brain just won't shut off. I've tried everything—no phone before bed, exercise, melatonin. Nothing really sticks.

Dr: That's really exhausting, and I can hear the frustration. When you wake up at two or three in the morning, what kinds of thoughts are running through your head? Are they specific worries, or more of a general sense of anxiety?

Client: *pauses, thinking* It's usually specific stuff. Like, I'll think about a presentation I have coming up and suddenly I'm analyzing every word I said in a meeting from last week. Or I'll start thinking about money—like, am I saving enough? What if something happens and I can't cover it? *laughs nervously* And then there's the relationship stuff with my partner. We've been together for three years, and lately I feel like we're just... existing in the same space, you know? I don't want to bring up the sleep thing or how stressed I am because I don't want to burden her.

Dr: You're carrying a lot right now—work concerns, financial worries, and also something in your relationship that feels delicate to address. Tell me more about what's happening with your partner. What does "existing in the same space" mean to you?

Client: *looks down* We don't really talk anymore. Not meaningfully. We'll sit on the couch together and watch TV, and it's fine, but I can't remember the last time we had a real conversation. I miss that. And I think she senses something's wrong with me, but I just... I don't know how to explain it without making her worry. She's got her own stuff going on at work. I don't want to dump this on her.

Dr: It sounds like you're trying to protect her, but in doing that, you might be creating some distance. Have you considered that she might actually want to know what's going on with you?

Client: *voice quieter* Yeah, I guess part of me knows that. But what if she thinks I'm... I don't know... broken? Or what if it makes things worse between us? We're supposed to be the stable ones, you know? Like, we're supposed to have it together by now.

Dr: Where does that "supposed to" come from? Who decided that you should have it all together by now?

Client: *long pause* Honestly? I think I did. And my parents. My dad was always very much a "push through it" kind of guy. Don't complain, just work harder. My mom worried about everything, so I learned early on not to add to that stress. So I became the person who handles things quietly. But I think... *voice catches slightly* I think I'm running out of steam, and I don't know how to ask for help.

Dr: That's a really important realization. You've built a pattern of managing things alone, and now you're noticing that it's not working the way it used to. What would it mean to ask for help—specifically from your partner?

Client: It would mean... admitting that I'm struggling. And I think that terrifies me, if I'm being honest. What if everything falls apart? What if she loses respect for me? *pauses* Though I guess things are kind of falling apart anyway, just quietly. At least if I said something, we'd be dealing with it together instead of me just suffering alone.

Dr: That's a real shift in perspective. And I'm curious—when you say things are falling apart, what does that actually look like? Are you having thoughts about your future, about whether things will get better?

Client: *hesitates* Sometimes I wonder if this is just... who I am now. Like, maybe I peaked and it's downhill from here. I'm thirty-two, and I feel like I should have more figured out. More energy. Better relationships. A clearer sense of direction. Instead, I feel stuck. And exhausted. And yeah, sometimes I think about how much easier it would be if I just... wasn't here to deal with any of it.

Dr: I'm glad you're telling me this. When you have those thoughts, how often do they come up, and do you ever think about acting on them?

Client: *shakes head* Not like that. I'm not planning anything, if that's what you're asking. It's more like... fleeting moments where I think, "Man, it would be nice to just not exist for a while." But I know that's not really what I want. I want things to feel better. I just don't know how to make that happen.

Dr: What you're describing—the sleep issues, the persistent heaviness, the loss of interest in things you used to enjoy, the isolation in your relationship, and these thoughts about not wanting to exist—these are significant things we should take seriously. I'd like to spend some time understanding this better and developing a plan with you. Does that feel okay?

Client: *nods slowly* Yeah. Yeah, I think I need that. I didn't expect to get emotional today, but... it actually helps to say this stuff out loud.

Dr: That's often how it works. Would you be open to talking about what's realistic for you moving forward—both in terms of what we do here and what you might want to address with your partner?

Client: I think so. I think I'm ready to try something different.

---

**[END OF SESSION SEGMENT]**

Client: बस... फोन उठा लेता हूँ। ईमेल चेक करता हूँ, व्हाट्सएप देखता हूँ। कभी-कभी ऑफिस के काम वाली चीजें ही देखता हूँ। फिर बस सोचता रहता हूँ कि आज क्या-क्या करना है। दिमाग बिल्कुल शांत नहीं होता।

Dr: समझ गया। तो यह चिंता का चक्र है जो रात भर चलता रहता है। अब बताओ, ऑफिस में क्या विशेष चीज़ है जो तुम्हें इतना सोचने पर मजबूर कर रही है?

Client: (लंबी सांस लेता है) दरअसल... तीन महीने पहले नई प्रोजेक्ट मिली है। यह बहुत बड़ी और महत्वपूर्ण है। कंपनी के लिए यह साल की सबसे बड़ी डील है। मुझे लीड दिया गया है।

Dr: अच्छा... तो लीड होने का मतलब है कि जिम्मेदारी बहुत है?

Client: हाँ, बहुत है। अगर यह प्रोजेक्ट सफल हो जाता है तो प्रमोशन मिल सकता है। लेकिन... (रुकता है) अगर कुछ गलत हुआ तो?

Dr: तो क्या हो सकता है अगर कुछ गलत हुआ?

Client: मुझे नहीं पता... मैं डर गया हूँ। कंपनी में बहुत लोग हैं जो मेरी जगह चाहते हैं। अगर यह प्रोजेक्ट फेल हो गई तो... मैं सोचता हूँ कि मुझे निकाल दिया जाएगा। घर पर पत्नी को यह सब बताया तो उसने कहा कि मैं बहुत नकारात्मक सोच रहा हूँ। पर मैं रोज़ ऑफिस में आता हूँ और देखता हूँ कि सब कुछ कितना प्रतिस्पर्धी है।

Dr: तुम्हारी पत्नी को लगता है कि तुम नकारात्मक सोच रहे हो। तुम्हें क्या लगता है?

Client: मुझे नहीं पता... मैं सिर्फ वास्तविकता देख रहा हूँ। (रुकता है) लेकिन शायद... शायद मैं सब कुछ को गलत तरीके से देख रहा हूँ। पिछली बार जब मुझे बड़ा प्रोजेक्ट मिला था, तो वह सफल रहा था। मुझे पदोन्नति भी मिली। पर इस बार मुझे डर लग रहा है। मैं जानता हूँ कि यह तर्कसंगत नहीं है।

Dr: तो तुम अपने पिछले सफलताओं के बारे में सोचते हो?

Client: हाँ, कभी-कभी। लेकिन तुरंत ही मुझे यह खयाल आता है कि शायद वह अलग था, या फिर इस बार परिस्थितियाँ अलग हैं। यह प्रोजेक्ट ज़्यादा जटिल है। टीम भी नई है। कुछ लोग अभी भी सीख रहे हैं।

Dr: (रुकता है) राज, एक बात पूछता हूँ - जब तुम रात को सोचते हो, तो क्या तुम्हें लगता है कि तुम इस स्थिति को संभाल सकते हो?

Client: (लंबा पौज) मुझे नहीं पता। कुछ दिन ऐसा लगता है कि हाँ, मैं कर सकता हूँ। लेकिन फिर कोई छोटी सी गलती हो जाती है - कोई ईमेल गलत भेज देता हूँ, या कोई deadline मिस हो जाती है - और तब मुझे लगता है कि मैं असफल हूँ।

Client: मुझे नहीं पता कहाँ से शुरू करूँ। हर दिन ऐसा ही लगता है।

Dr: ठीक है। अगले सत्र में हम कुछ व्यावहारिक तकनीकें सीखेंगे...
